driver.body: The architecture is necessary but not sufficient. A pipe that carries six streams to six destinations is still a conglomerate with good plumbing — real infrastructure, no moat. The flywheel is the sharper claim: each domain's data raises the value of every other domain's data **super-additively**, so breadth B is not the sum of six datasets but their product on shared keys [S-085]. Driver 2 is the mechanism of that compounding, and its centrepiece is the data-flow Sankey and the flywheel network graph — the two pictures that show the multiplication rather than the addition, and the place where the moat identity M = D × B × L × T either earns its multiplication sign or does not.

mechanisms.body:
The compounding is concrete, and it lives in specific joins no single-vertical firm can compute. **S-605**: D1 location traces, D5 accelerometer telemetry, and D3 courier GPS join on geohash × t to produce two features — `road_roughness` and `curb_availability`. Neither is a curiosity. Road_roughness improves D1 routing and D3 drop-off ETA at the same time, and curb_availability feeds D2 charger-siting, because rough approach roads depress charger utilisation [S-086]. No competitor with only a rideshare app, or only a delivery app, can compute either feature — none holds all three streams on one key, so the join simply does not exist inside their data.

**S-606** is the second join, and it points at trust rather than routing. D4 authorisation outcomes join to D1 rider history and D5 device fingerprint to lift the fraud model. A card-decline pattern that reads as noise in isolation becomes explicable once the same entity's mobility regularity and device health are columns in the same row — a legitimate rider on a healthy device is a different risk than the identical decline attached to neither [S-086].

The data-flow Sankey makes the accumulation visible: the six sources are the left band, the moat is the single right node, and each ribbon's width is the share of the moat's feature value that source contributes. That last line is the whole argument in one number. The **+19% cross-join uplift** is feature value that literally cannot exist for a focused specialist, because producing it requires two streams the specialist does not both own [S-086]. It is the quantified answer to the SCQA's question — the compounding is real, not a story, and it is measurable as the fraction of served feature value that lives only on joins.

If the Sankey shows how much each domain feeds the moat, the network graph shows that every feed is also a return — the loop that makes the wheel a wheel. This is the single most important picture in the section: the six domains as nodes, the moat as the hub, and directed edges running in both directions — raw data in, models out [S-087].

**S-607**: the graph is directed, and the strategic content lives in the return edges, not the input ones.

- D1 sends traces and receives routing and safety models that raise density D by cutting deadhead miles **~11%**.
- D4 sends fraud and settlement signals and receives a credit model that raises trust T — which is precisely what lets D1 and D3 run cash-free.
- D5 sends proprietary sensor telemetry — the breadth B no competitor holds — and receives OTA and health models back.

Delete the hub and the picture collapses into six unconnected nodes. That collapse is the visual proof of the governing thought: the conglomerate discount is the market pricing the six nodes on a sum-of-the-parts basis; the flywheel is the edges the market cannot see, because a return edge never shows up in any single domain's standalone P&L [S-087].

The flywheel's output is economic, and the number is the one the entire document is anchored to: blended contribution per interaction rising from **−0.31** over ten years. Mechanism 2.3 attributes that swing to the moat rather than to any one domain's margin [S-088].

**S-608**: each 10% rise in served feature value — mostly the cross-join band from Mechanism 2.1 — lowers three marginal costs at once: the cost of a mobility decision (fewer empty miles), of a logistics match (higher fill), and of a payment authorisation (fewer false declines). They fall together because all three read the same improved features [S-088]. That simultaneity is the point. One investment in breadth pays out across four domains at once, which is why Domain 6's **−$420m** is not a cost centre but the price of the multiplier — the line item that buys the multiplication sign in M = D × B × L × T. Driver 3 is how that feature value becomes decisions [S-088].
